Variable_Description: The value in each model segment shows the number of GCMs (zero to thirteen) where the historical GCM distribution is statistically similar to the reference (Maurer) simulation during the historical period (1952-2005) for a given simulation type and PRMS metric.
Variable_Methodology: Statistical significance is calculated using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test (KS test) using a threshold of alpha = 0.05 (Massey, 1951). In a previous version of this dataset "a particular GCM was considered acceptable for representing a streamflow statistic for historical conditions if at least 75 percent of the modeling units (HRU or stream segments) did not reject the null  hypothesis of the KS test at an alpha of 0.05 when compared to the observation-based streamflow statistics. A streamflow statistic was considered acceptable overall if at least 10 of the GCMs passed the 75 percent criteria" (LaFontaine and others, 2019).
Variable_Caveats: Values are presented at each individual stream segment and therefore no threshold was adopted for whether a given model or statistic is acceptable over a region. Users should assess the fitness of a metric for their region of interest and may adopt the threshold detailed above as a benchmark. The choice of KS testing is generally useful for these applications given the distribution and number of observations (n=30) for each dataset. Users should maintain a consistent application of models and user should NOT limit their analysis to only those models that show acceptable performance.
Additional_User_Information: Statistics that do not pass this test may not be as reliable. However, this test assumes that the reference (Maurer) simulation is "truth" and users should investigate the reliability of the reference simulation in their area of interest through comparison with observation when available (see Plot Visualization Tab).
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** Massey, F. J., 1951 The Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test for Goodness of Fit: Journal of the American Statistical Association, v. 46, no. 253, p. 68-78. https://doi.org/10.2307/2280095

Variable_Description: The value in each model segment shows the number of GCMs (zero to thirteen) where the historical GCM distribution (1976-2005) is statistically different to a future period (2016-2045, 2046-2075, 2071-2100) in the same GCM for a given simulation type, emissions scenario, and PRMS metric.
Variable_Methodology: Statistical significance is calculated using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test (KS test) using a threshold of alpha = 0.05 (Massey, 1951; LaFontaine et al. 2019). If a model fails to reject the null hypothesis then the historical and future results could have been drawn from the same probability distribution and there is not a significant change in the distribution from the historical to future period.
Variable_Caveats: Values are presented at each individual stream segment and therefore no threshold was adopted for whether a given model or statistic is acceptable over a region. Users should assess the fitness of a metric for their region of interest. Users should NOT limit their analysis to only those models that show a significant change. The choice of KS testing is generally useful for these applications given the distribution and number of observations (n=30) for each dataset.
Additional_User_Information: Statistics that show significant change may be seen in models with large changes in temperature and precipitation among the full ensemble. This metric is provided to users to contextualize changes across time and highlight where changes become statistically significant.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** Massey, F. J., 1951 The Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test for Goodness of Fit: Journal of the American Statistical Association, v. 46, no. 253, p. 68-78. https://doi.org/10.2307/2280095

Variable_Description: The value in each model segment shows the number of GCMs (zero to thirteen) that show an increase in the metric from the historical (1976-2005) to the future period (2016-2045, 2046-2075, 2071-2100) in the same GCM for a given simulation type, emissions scenario, and PRMS metric.
Variable_Methodology: Model agreement is calculated by summing the number of models projected to have a positive change in the metric for each time period, simulation type, emissions scenario, and PRMS metric for each individual model segment. Note that this metric shows the number of models that agree on a positive numeric change in the metric. Therefore values on either end of the distribution show high model agreement, for example, 13 indicates that all models indicate a positive numeric change in the metric and a 0 indicates that all models indicate a negative numeric change in the metric.
Variable_Caveats: High model agreement does not necessarily indicate that the projected changes are large or statistically significant relative to the historical distribution (see Future Change Significance).
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ.

Variable_Description: This layer indicates segments where at least one season has an average flow of less than 10 cfs in the reference (Maurer) simulation during the historical period (1976-2005) for a given simulation type. Segments with low flow are indicated with a 1 while segments that do not exhibit low flow as defined here are indicated with a 0.
Variable_Methodology: Seasonal average flow was calculated from the monthly mean metrics (ma12-ma23) for October-December, January-March, April-June, and July-September for the reference (Maurer) simulation.
Variable_Caveats: Averaging over a three month period and over the historical period as a whole may miss particularly low flow years or low flow within a single month, excluding some stream segments from the low flow classification. The threshold of 10 cfs is subjective, and other low flow thresholds may be more appropriate for some locations or applications.
Additional_User_Information: Users should use caution when looking at change metrics in these streams given the very high (un-physical) changes seen, especially for low-flow metrics. This classification may also miss zero flow streams based on observations. The classification is performed for the Maurer reference dataset and therefore some GCMs may also have extremely low flow simulated that is not captured by this methodology. Users should examine the monthly mean flow values (shown in the Plot Visualization Tab) and use caution if low flow thresholds are crossed.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ.

Variable_Caveats: Averaging over a three month period and over the historical period as a whole may miss particularly low flow years or low flow within a single month, excluding some stream segments from the low flow classification. Variable_Description: This layer indicates segments where at least one season has an average flow of less than 0.1 cfs in the reference (Maurer) simulation during the historical period (1976-2005) for a given simulation type. Segments with low flow are indicated with a 1 while segments that do not exhibit low flow as defined here are indicated with a 0.
Variable_Methodology: Seasonal average flow was calculated from the monthly mean metrics (ma12-ma23) for October-December, January-March, April-June, and July-September for the reference (Maurer) simulation.
Variable_Caveats: Averaging over a three month period and over the historical period as a whole may miss particularly low flow years or low flow within a single month, excluding some stream segments from the low flow classification. The threshold of 0.1 cfs is subjective, and other low flow thresholds may be more appropriate for some locations or applications. This flow threshold is also adopted in the PRMS metric LF1.
Additional_User_Information: Users should use caution when looking at change metrics in these streams given the very high (un-physical) changes seen, especially for low-flow metrics. This classification may also miss zero flow streams based on observations. The classification is performed for the Maurer reference dataset and therefore some GCMs may also have extremely low flow simulated that is not captured by this methodology. Users should examine the monthly mean flow values (shown in the Plot Visualization Tab) and use caution if low flow thresholds are crossed.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ.

Variable_Description: The value shown is the land cover classification for the historical period (1952-2005) identified in the time invariant parameter files. Categories are 0 = bare ground, 1 = grass, 2 =  shrub/scrub, 3 = trees.
Variable_Methodology: Land cover classification (cov_type) is a PRMS parameter assigned to each hydrologic response unit (hru) (LaFontaine et al. 2023b). Here we identify the hru's that directly contribute to each stream segment and assign the land cover type that covers the most contributing area to each segment. "These parameters were developed from the raw land-cover values from the EROS grids and summarized for each HRU. The five hydrologic parameters were derived from both historical and potential future data sources (Sohl and others, 2014, 2016). The historical dataset consisted of (1) annual time step historical land-cover estimates for 1992-2005 from the National Assessment of Ecosystem Carbon and Greenhouse Gas Fluxes project, and (2) annual time step land-cover data for 1938-1992 based on best available tabular and statistical data, including data from the Agriculture Census, Population Census, USGS Land Cover Trends, and other sources (Sohl and others, 2014, 2016). In the static simulation, Dominant land-cover type and vegetation density parameters were computed by Regan and others (2018) using the National Land Cover Database 2001 (NLCD2001; Homer and others, 2007)" (La Fontaine et al., 2019).
Variable_Caveats: Historical/static land cover is anchored to 2001.
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB.

Variable_Description: The value shown is the percent of impervious area for the historical period (1952-2005) identified in the time invariant parameter files (between 0 and 1). A larger value indicates a higher percentage of the associated hru is classified as impervious area.
Variable_Methodology: Percent impervious area (percent_imperv) is a PRMS parameter assigned to each hydrologic response unit (hru) (LaFontaine et al. 2023b). Here we identify the hru's that directly contribute to each stream segment and assign an area weighted value to each segment. "Overland runoff parameters describing percent impervious area, maximum contributing area, and runoff fraction were computed using the NLCD2001, the NHDPlus version 1 DEM and waterbodies coverage, and estimates of Topographic Wetness Index (Beven and Kirby, 1979)" ​(LaFontaine et al. 2019).
Variable_Caveats: Historical/static land cover is anchored to 2001.
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB.

Variable_Description: This value indicates the change in land cover from the historical (1976-2005) to the future period (2016-2045, 2046-2075, 2071-2100) for the dynamic simulation. The land cover classes are designated as 0 = bare ground, 1 = grass, 2 =  shrub/scrub, 3 = trees. If the change is positive, the land cover changed to a more vegetated type; if the change is negative, the land cover changed to a less vegetated type. A value of zero means that the cover type remained the same.
Variable_Methodology: Land cover classification (cov_type) is a PRMS parameter assigned to each hydrologic response unit (hru) (LaFontaine et al. 2023b). Here we identify the hru's that directly contribute to each stream segment and assign the land cover type that covers the most contributing area to each segment. The land cover type that exists for the most years in the period is used (i.e. the mode). "The potential future land-cover estimates for 2006-2050 were simulated by Sohl and others (2014) using the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Special Report on Emissions Scenarios A1B emissions scenario as part of the National Assessment of Ecosystem Carbon and Greenhouse Gas Fluxes project" (La Fontaine et al., 2019).
Variable_Caveats: User needs to be aware that the future land cover is projected using the A1B emissions scenario and therefore may not necessarily align with the climate projections being used as input for temperature and precipitation (RCP).
Additional_User_Information: Areas that experience drastic changes in land cover type can lead to significant changes in runoff. Users should be aware of areas that have significant land use change when considering static vs. dynamic projections.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB.

Variable_Description: This value indicates the change in impervious area from the historical (1976-2005) to the future period (2016-2045, 2046-2075, 2071-2100) for the dynamic simulation. A positive number indicates the percentage of impervious area increased in the future.
Variable_Methodology: Percent impervious area (percent_imperv) is a PRMS parameter assigned to each hydrologic response unit (hru) (LaFontaine et al. 2023b). Here we identify the hru's that directly contribute to each stream segment and assign an area weighted value to each segment.  The average impervious area for the period is used. "Overland runoff parameters describing percent impervious area, maximum contributing area, and runoff fraction were computed using the NLCD2001, the NHDPlus version 1 DEM and waterbodies coverage, and estimates of Topographic Wetness Index (Beven and Kirby, 1979)" ​(LaFontaine et al. 2019).
Variable_Caveats: User needs to be aware that the future land cover is projected using the A1B emissions scenario and therefore may not necessarily align with the climate projections being used as input for temperature and precipitation (RCP).
Additional_User_Information: Areas that experience drastic changes in land cover type can lead to significant changes in runoff. Users should be aware of areas that have significant land use change when considering static vs. dynamic projections. Increases in impervious area can lead to reduced infiltration and increases in the number and magnitude of storm runoff events. This can also impact the calculation of model evapotranspiration.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB.

Variable_Description: This layer indicates segments with a USGS stream gage (Hay and Lafontaine et al. 2020; LaFontaine et al. 2023b). A stream segment with the value 1 indicates that there is a stream gage co-located with this segment (according to the GAGES-II dataset (Falcone et al. 2011)).
Variable_Methodology: The gages included were utilized in the calibration of PRMS using the Maurer reference data (LaFontaine et al. 2023b). The R-package DataRetrieval was used to download daily flow, site information, and parameter information for each gage (De Cicco et al. 2025).
Variable_Caveats: Note that not all gages have data for the full observational period or an uninterupted data record. For more information on the observed flow record, please see the Plot Visualization tab.
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB. *** De Cicco, L.A., Hirsch, R.M., Lorenz, D., Watkins, W.D., Johnson, M., 2025, dataRetrieval: R packages for discovering and retrieving water data available from Federal hydrologic web services, v.2.7.19, doi:10.5066/P9X4L3GE. *** Falcone, J., 2011, GAGES-II: Geospatial Attributes of Gages for Evaluating Streamflow: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P96CPHOT

Variable_Description: The value shown is the calculated Nash Sutcliffe Efficiency of the observed daily streamflow and the reference (Maurer) daily streamflow between 1951-2009.
Variable_Methodology: This metric was calculated using the daily streamflow timeseries from 1951-2009 using the NSE function in the HydroGOF R package (Zambrano-Bigiarini, 2024).
Variable_Caveats: Analysis shown may not be adequate to fully assess the data reliability. E.g., in Hay et al. 2023 the NSE is performed on  daily, log-daily and monthly flow data and does not necessarily reflect accuracy at the extremes of the flow distribution.
Additional_User_Information: The threshold indicated here is 0.5 which is indicated as the acceptable threshold in Lafontaine et al. 2019. Gages with a Nash Sutcliffe Efficiency greater than 0.5 were considered acceptable. This aligns with USACE recommendations (https://www.hec.usace.army.mil/confluence/hmsdocs/hmstrm/calibration/calibration-summary-statistics).
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB. *** Zambrano-Bigiarini, Mauricio (2024). hydroGOF: Goodness-of-fit functions for comparison of simulated and observed hydrological time series. R package version 0.6-0. URL:https://cran.r-project.org/package=hydroGOF. doi:10.5281/zenodo.839854.

Variable_Description: The value shown is the calculated Percent Bias of the observed daily streamflow and the reference (Maurer) daily streamflow between 1951-2009.
Variable_Methodology: This metric was calculated using the daily streamflow timeseries from 1951-2009 using the Pbias function in the HydroGOF R package (Zambrano-Bigiarini, 2024).
Variable_Caveats: Analysis shown may not be adequate to fully assess the data reliability.
Additional_User_Information: The threshold indicated here is 25 which is indicated as the acceptable threshold in Lafontaine et al. 2019. Gages with an absolute percent bias less than 25 were considered acceptable. This aligns with USACE recommendations (https://www.hec.usace.army.mil/confluence/hmsdocs/hmstrm/calibration/calibration-summary-statistics).

R package version 0.6-0. URL:https://cran.r-project.org/package=hydroGOF. doi:10.5281/zenodo.839854.

Variable_Description: The value shown is the calculated Ratio of the Root Mean Square Error to the Standard Deviation Ratio (RSR) of the observed daily streamflow and the reference (Maurer) daily streamflow between 1951-2009.
Variable_Methodology: This metric was calculated using the daily streamflow timeseries from 1951-2009 using the rsr function in the HydroGOF R package (Zambrano-Bigiarini, 2024).
Variable_Caveats: Analysis shown may not be adequate to fully assess the data reliability.
Additional_User_Information: The threshold indicated here is 0.7 which is indicated as the acceptable threshold in Lafontaine et al. 2019. Gages with a Ratio of RMSE to the Standard Deviation of the Observations less than 0.7 were considered acceptable. This aligns with USACE recommendations (https://www.hec.usace.army.mil/confluence/hmsdocs/hmstrm/calibration/calibration-summary-statistics). Please note that this metric is called NRMSE in LaFontaine et al. 2019 and Hay and LaFontaine, 2020 but the calculation aligns with RSR.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB. *** Zambrano-Bigiarini, Mauricio (2024). hydroGOF: Goodness-of-fit functions for comparison of simulated and observed hydrological time series. R package version 0.6-0. URL:https://cran.r-project.org/package=hydroGOF. doi:10.5281/zenodo.839854.

Variable_Description: This value gives a composite score for reference (Maurer) simulation performance from 1951-2009 using a composite score of NSE, Pbias, and RSR. A gage is scored from 0-1 = Poor, 2 = Fair, 3 = Good.
Variable_Methodology: Gages receive a skill point if they pass a validation threshold for each of the validation metrics. These threshold are adopted using thresholds applied for analysis in LaFontaine et al. 2019. Gages received one point each for having NSE > 0.5, absolute Pbias < 25, and RSR < 0.7 for a maximum of 3 points.
Variable_Caveats: Analysis shown may not be adequate to fully assess the data reliability. E.g., in Hay et al. 2023 the NSE is performed on  daily, log-daily and monthly flow data and does not necessarily reflect accuracy at the extremes of the flow distribution.
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., and Riley, J.W., 2023a, Model Input and Output for Hydrologic Simulations for the Conterminous United States for Historical and Future Conditions Using the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure (NHM) and the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5), 1950-2100: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9EBKREQ. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB. *** Zambrano-Bigiarini, Mauricio (2024). hydroGOF: Goodness-of-fit functions for comparison of simulated and observed hydrological time series. R package version 0.6-0. URL:https://cran.r-project.org/package=hydroGOF. doi:10.5281/zenodo.839854.

Variable_Description: This layer indicates, for segments that have a stream gage (Hay and Lafontaine et al. 2020; LaFontaine et al. 2023b), what the associated disturbance index is according to the GAGES-II dataset.
Variable_Methodology: Disturbance Index based on the GAGES-II dataset (Falcone, 2011) and collected in the Daymet 3 PRMS calibration reference (Hay and LaFontaine, 2020). Values increase with anthropogenic hydologic modification.
Variable_Caveats: Note that the reference or non-reference status may not reflect recent conditions. Please see additional information about GAGES-II to understand the limitations of this designation.
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** Hay, L.E., and LaFontaine, J.H., 2020, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS),1980-2016, Daymet Version 3 calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9PGZE0S. *** Hay, L.E., LaFontaine, J.H., Van Beusekom, A.E., Norton, P.A., Farmer, W.H., Regan, R.S., Markstrom, S.L., and Dickinson, J.E., 2023, Parameter estimation at the conterminous United States scale and streamflow routing enhancements for the National Hydrologic Model infrastructure application of the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS): U.S. Geological Survey Techniques and Methods 6–B10, 50 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/tm6B10. *** Falcone, J., 2011, GAGES-II: Geospatial Attributes of Gages for Evaluating Streamflow: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P96CPHOT

Variable_Description: This layer indicates, for segments that have a stream gage (Hay and Lafontaine et al. 2020; LaFontaine et al. 2023b), whether the stream gage is a reference or non-reference gage in the GAGES-II dataset (Falcone, 2011). Segments indicated with a 1 have a coincident stream gage that is considered a reference gage. Segments indicated with a 0 have a coincident stream gage that is considered a non-reference gage.
Variable_Methodology: Reference or Non-reference status based on the GAGES-II dataset (Falcone, 2011) and collected in the Daymet 3 PRMS calibration reference (Hay and LaFontaine, 2020).
Variable_Caveats: Note that the reference or non-reference status may not reflect recent conditions. Please see additional information about GAGES-II to understand the limitations of this designation.
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** Hay, L.E., and LaFontaine, J.H., 2020, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS),1980-2016, Daymet Version 3 calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9PGZE0S. *** Hay, L.E., LaFontaine, J.H., Van Beusekom, A.E., Norton, P.A., Farmer, W.H., Regan, R.S., Markstrom, S.L., and Dickinson, J.E., 2023, Parameter estimation at the conterminous United States scale and streamflow routing enhancements for the National Hydrologic Model infrastructure application of the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS): U.S. Geological Survey Techniques and Methods 6–B10, 50 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/tm6B10. *** Falcone, J., 2011, GAGES-II: Geospatial Attributes of Gages for Evaluating Streamflow: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P96CPHOT

Variable_Description: This value indicates the Modified Strahler Stream Order of each segment (NHDPlusV2.1).
Variable_Methodology: Stream order is obtained from the National Hydrologic Dataset (NHDPlusV2.1) using QGIS to join attributes. Because the NHD segment resolution is higher than the PRMS dataset, the stream order assigned to each stream segment is the stream order of the largest overlapping NHD segment. The uppermost channels in a drainage network (i.e., headwater channels with no upstream tributaries) are designated as first-order streams down to their first confluence (Strahler, 1957).
Variable_Caveats: N/A
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** Strahler, Arthur N. 1957. Quantitative Analysis of Watershed Geomorphology: Transactions, American Geophysical Union, Vol. 38, no. 6. https://doi.org/10.1029/TR038i006p00913

Variable_Description: This value indicates a U.S. Stream Classification System category (McManamay and DeRolph, 2019).
Variable_Methodology: Stream Classification is obtained from McManamay and DeRolph, 2019. Classifications are calculated using Ward's agglomerative hydrologic classes (14 clusters) at the National Hydrologic Dataset (NHDPlusV2.1) resolution. PRMS level classification was developed using QGIS to join attributes. Because the NHD segment resolution is higher than the PRMS dataset, the stream classification assigned to each stream segment is the longest overlapping stream classification.
Variable_Caveats: The choice of the fourteen class cluster is subjective and may not be a suitable granularity for analysis. McManamay and DeRolph, 2019 provide additional Ward's agglomerative clustering solutions for 2, 4, eight, and thirty classes.
Additional_User_Information: Stream classifications based on historical flow regime and temperature have commonly been used within the ecological flow community to apply broad characteristics across regions that might have limited data. These classifications, based on size, gradient, hydrology, temperature, bifurcation, and valley confinement allow users to connect flow change to other characteristics or classifications that may be relevant.
Variable_References: *** McManamay, Ryan; DeRolph, Chris (2019). A Stream Classification System for the Conterminous United States. figshare. Collection. https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.c.4233740.v1. *** McManamay, R., DeRolph, C. A stream classification system for the conterminous United States. Sci Data 6, 190017 (2019). https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2019.17

Variable_Description: This value shows the Native Aquatic Vulnerability Index (Panlasigui et al. 2018). The vulnerability of an area increases with the index.
Variable_Methodology: Native Aquatic Species Vulnerability (NAVI) illustrates a measure of the overall vulnerability of native aquatic animal species (amphibians, fish, mollusks, decapods, and turtles). The Vulnerability Index for a watershed is a normalized average of two metrics, the Rarity Index (which assesses the average rarity of the species in a watershed based on their ranges across the U.S., with small range species being considered rare) and the count of threatened and endangered species in the watershed. A segment value is obtained by the maxim NAVI from an overlapping HUC12 for each stream segment.
Variable_Caveats: This dataset was developed in 2018 and may not reflect current ecological conditions. This index also uses the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) designations and may not align with categorizations used by state and federal entities.
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** Panlasigui, S., A.J.S. Davis, M.J. Mangiante, and J.A. Darling. 2018. Assessing threats of non-native species to native freshwater biodiversity: Conservation priorities for the United States. Biological Conservation 224: 199–208.

Variable_Description: This layer indicates whether a stream segment intersects with a region designated as Critical Habitat by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. Segments indicated with a 1 intersect with areas designated as critical habitat. Segments indicated with a 0 do not intersect with areas designated as critical habitat. The entire segment may not overlap with critical habitat areas.
Variable_Methodology: The critical habitat layer was downloaded here (https://ecos.fws.gov/ecp/report/table/critical-habitat.html).
Variable_Caveats: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife service notes that not all of the critical habitat data designated by the Fish & Wildlife Service is available from this portal and may need to be obtained from the FWD Lead Region for the species.
Additional_User_Information: We do not make any distinctions in the critical habitat, including the species or purpose of designation or whether the habitat is proposed or final. Therefore some segments may be indicated as critical habitat where the species in question is terrestrial.
Variable_References: N/A

Variable_Description: Value indicates an EPA Level II Eco-Region. Codes are available in the associated literature.
Variable_Methodology: Shapefiles for the Eco-Region are obtained from the EPA here (https://www.epa.gov/eco-research/ecoregions-north-america) and are consistent with the level of analysis in Lafontaine et al. 2019. A segment category is determined by the longest overlap with a given region.
Variable_Caveats: The choice of Level II Eco-Regions is subjective and may not be a suitable granularity for analysis.
Additional_User_Information: Eco-Regions have commonly been used within the ecological flow community to apply broad characteristics across regions that might have limited data and provide a potential method to generalize flow-ecology relationships.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039.

Variable_Description: Value indicates the PRMS segment type.
Variable_Methodology: Values are obtained from the PRMS parameter files (LaFontaine et al. 2023b) (0=segment; 1= headwater; 2=lake; 3=replace inflow; 4=inbound to NHM; 5=outbound from NHM; 6=inbound to region; 7=outbound from region; 8=drains to ocean; 9=sink; 10=inbound from Great Lakes; 11=outbound to Great Lakes, add 100 to flag that the value is updated).
Variable_Caveats: N/A
Additional_User_Information: There is no clear guidance around segment type and output interpretation, but users should be aware when a given segment type is non-standard and may warrant more investigation.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB.

Variable_Description: Value indicates the cumulative area (acres) contributing to inflow for a given segment, including direct contribution and upstream collection. Segments with a value of zero are highlighted.
Variable_Methodology: Values are obtained from the PRMS parameter files (LaFontaine et al. 2023b).
Variable_Caveats: N/A
Additional_User_Information: There is no clear guidance around segment cumulative area and output interpretation, but users should be aware when a given segment  is non-standard and may warrant more investigation. Some segments may have zero cumulative area because of their location at the beginning of the network.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB.

Variable_Description: Value indicates the direct area (acres) contributing to inflow for a given segment. Segments with a value of zero are highlighted.
Variable_Methodology: Values are obtained from the PRMS parameter files (LaFontaine et al. 2023b) and claculated by summing the area of hrus that directly contribute to a given segment.
Variable_Caveats: N/A
Additional_User_Information: There is no clear guidance around segment direct contributing area and output interpretation, but users should be aware when a given segment  is non-standard and may warrant more investigation. Segments with zero contributing area in the middle of  the network are used as connector segments between model zones and will inherit the same values as their preceding segment. Users should be aware of these segments when including them in an analysis area.
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB.

Variable_Description: Value indicates the average slope (m/m) for the segment.
Variable_Methodology: Values are obtained from the PRMS parameters files (LaFontaine et al. 2023b).
Variable_Caveats: N/A
Additional_User_Information: N/A
Variable_References: *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hart, R.M., Hay, L.E., Farmer, W.H., Bock, A.R., Viger, R.J., Markstrom, S.L., Regan, R.S., and Driscoll, J.M., 2019, Simulation of water availability in the Southeastern United States for historical and potential future climate and land-cover conditions: U.S. Geological Survey Scientific Investigations Report 2019-5039, 83 p., https://doi.org/10.3133/sir20195039. *** LaFontaine, J.H., Hay, L.E., and Riley, J.R., 2023b, Application of the National Hydrologic Model Infrastructure with the Precipitation-Runoff Modeling System (NHM-PRMS), 1950-2010, Maurer Calibration: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9CVHLMB.

Variable_Description: Value indicates whether the segment intersects with a man-made connection described in the National Hydrologic Dataset (NHDPlusV2.1) and the nature of the connection.
Variable_Methodology: Segments are assigned either a null value if they do not intersect with a man-made connection (0) or are assigned a value of 1 if they intersect with a segment of type CanalDitch, Connector, or Pipeline (FTYPE/FCODE in NHDPlusV2.1).
Variable_Caveats: N/A
Additional_User_Information: This value may alert users to areas where there is significant alteration in the natural flow network or in the network represented in PRMS that may impact observed flows. In some cases, connections may indicate where additional water is leaving or entering the system that is note represented in the model.
Variable_References: N/A
